behaviors.description: Only files changed in the pull request are analyzed — unchanged files are not scanned
behaviors.justification: A pure Unit test of the file-filtering logic could pass even if the code forgets to call the filter (scanning all files instead). The real failure—'code reads all files from the project instead of just PR changes'—only appears when mocking the GitHub API and checking which files actually get opened/scanned from the filesystem. This requires wiring the GitHub API mock with actual (or simulated) filesystem state to catch the false positive.
behaviors.test_description: Mock GitHub API to return a specific set of changed files (e.g., [file_a.py, file_b.py]), create a test filesystem with additional unchanged files, run the linter, and verify that only the changed files were analyzed by checking file-open/read calls or lint results.

behaviors.description: Findings are grouped by severity (error, warning, info) in the posted PR comment
behaviors.justification: The grouping/formatting logic is pure data transformation and can be tested by mocking the linter output. However, mocking just the linter and verifying the grouping function in isolation (Unit) risks missing failures in how the grouped output is formatted into the actual PR comment. Integration-level testing—mock linter output, execute the full formatting pipeline, verify the PR comment structure—catches both the grouping and formatting logic together.
behaviors.test_description: Mock the linter output with findings of mixed severity (some errors, some warnings, some info), run the output-formatting step, and verify the resulting PR comment has findings grouped under severity headers or sections (e.g., '### Errors', '### Warnings') with no interleaving.

behaviors.description: A pull request that touches 200 files completes analysis within the 10-minute Actions timeout
behaviors.justification: Mocking file I/O or linter execution masks the real failure: actual linter subprocess latency, real filesystem reads, and real rule execution over 200 files. Only running the linter as a real subprocess (or very close simulation) with real file I/O and measuring elapsed time reveals timeout failures. This is a System-level concern because the failure boundary is OS/subprocess behavior and real I/O latency, not module wiring.
behaviors.test_description: Set up a test environment with 200 real (or realistic) files, invoke the linter subprocess, measure end-to-end execution time, and assert completion within 600 seconds (10 minutes). Track whether file reads, rule execution, and result collection all complete in time.

behaviors.description: When the linter process crashes on a malformed file, the remaining files are still analyzed and results posted
behaviors.justification: Error handling for subprocess crashes is a System-level concern. Mocking the linter exception in-process (Integration) misses the real failure mode: a subprocess exit code or signal that is not caught by the parent orchestration code. The real failure is 'subprocess crashes, parent doesn't catch it, whole pipeline stops and posts no comment'. Catching this requires invoking the linter as a real subprocess and verifying the parent handles the crash gracefully.
behaviors.test_description: Create a malformed or pathological file that causes the linter subprocess to crash or exit with an error code, run the orchestration code, verify it catches the subprocess error, continues analyzing remaining files, and still posts a partial result to the PR (with results from non-crashing files and a note about the failure).

behaviors.description: Duplicate findings on the same line from overlapping rules are deduplicated in the output
behaviors.justification: Deduplication is pure data transformation logic that can be tested by mocking the linter output. A Unit test of the deduplication function alone is possible, but Integration-level testing—mock linter output with realistic duplicates, run the dedup + output pipeline—ensures the logic works end-to-end and catches edge cases in how duplicates are identified and merged.
behaviors.test_description: Mock linter output with duplicate findings (e.g., both rule_A and rule_B flag line 42 with similar issues), pass it through the deduplication logic, and verify the output contains only one finding per line (or merged findings if the dedup merges rather than removes).
